family: Replication probe (serves R7 / R12; resolves spark-06)

replay_proof.method: Replay with every ALLOWLIST entry monkeypatched to raise, socket.connect patched to raise, GROQ_API_KEY absent; 3 observations replayed, 4 events reproduced, 0 adapter calls.

unresolved_questions: Does kinship/motif vocabulary pull as hard as address vocabulary? That is A6's actual exposure and this run says nothing about it.
Would a counterbalanced order (arm B first) reproduce the asymmetry?
Is 'address' special because it names a viewer-relation, or would any relational frame do?
notes: A/B replication resolving spark-06. Same image, identical 768px stimulus by sha256 in both arms, same model/budget/call-shape, both stateless; only the question frame differed. ARM A (address-framed) produced 'glowing eyes', 'face-like frontality', 'almost facial architecture'. ARM B (structure-framed) described the SAME lamps as 'light fixtures', 'mirrored placement', 'visual anchors'. Pre-registered cell a_face_b_none obtained: THE QUESTION FRAME DRIVES IT. Supersedes A4's narrowing, which rested on a negative result on a different image that may have lacked the triggering feature. Secondary result: arm A reproduced A3's face reading at 768px single-image, so A3's finding was NOT an artifact of 256px degradation or 3-image comparison context. Scope stated narrowly: address vocabulary resolved; kinship/motif vocabulary explicitly NOT, which is what A6 actually asks.
